content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

september 9, 1991

Cheryl J. Green
Executive Director
Telephone Answering Services of
California Inc.
5000 Van Nuys Boulevard, Suite 300
Sherman Oaks, CA 91403

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. A-91-383

Dear Ms. Green:

This letter is in response to your request for advice on
behalf of Telephone Answering Services of California Inc.
regarding the campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
"Act").1/

QUESTIONS

1. May funds deposited in the organization's sponsored
political action committee account be transferred to the
organization's general operation account upon termination of the
committee?

2. What are the procedures for terminating a political
action committee?

CONCLUSIONS

1. Funds deposited in the committee's account may not be
transferred to the organization's general operation account.

2. Political action committees which meet the criteria
described in the following analysis may terminate their filing
obligations by filing a Statement of Termination (Form 415).

_________________________

1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Cheryl J. Green
Page 2


FACTS

The Telephone Answering Services of California Inc. sponsors
the TASC-PAC (Telephone Answering Services of California Political
Action Committee), a committee formed in 1980. The committee
wants to terminate and transfer approximately $7,000 of committee
funds into the organization's general operation account.

ANALYSIS

Effective January 1, 1990, the Act placed statutory
restrictions on the use of campaign funds. (Sections 89510-
89521.) Campaign funds include any contributions, cash, cash
equivalents and other assets received or possessed by a sponsored
committee. Generally speaking, any expenditure of campaign funds
must be, at a minimum, reasonably related to a political,
legislative, or governmental purpose. (Section 89512.) A
transfer of funds by a sponsored committee to the sponsor's
general operation account does not appear to serve a political,
governmental, or legislative purpose. (Brown Advice Letter, No.
I-90-412, copy enclosed.)

A committee may terminate its filing obligations if all of
the following criteria are met:

(a) The committee has ceased to receive contributions and
make expenditures;

(b) The committee does not anticipate receiving
contributions, repayments of outstanding loans made to others, or
any other receipts in the future and does not anticipate making
expenditures in the future;

(c) The committee has eliminated or has declared that it has
no intention or ability to discharge all of its debts, loans
received and other obligations;

(d) The committee has no surplus funds; and

(e) The committee has filed all of its required campaign
statements disclosing all reportable transactions.

Regulation 18404.

Cheryl J. Green
Page 3




     If all of the above criteria for terminating a recipient
committee are met, the committee may terminate by filing a Form
415 (Statement of Termination) with a final campaign statement,
Form 420 or 450.

     If you have additional questions concerning this matter,
please contact me at 916/322-5662.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Scott Hallabrin
                                        Acting General Counsel

                                        [signature]
                                   By:  Mary Ann Kvasager
                                        Political Reform Consultant

Enclosure
